title: The burden of disease vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: The burden of disease is measured in Disability-Adjusted Life Years (DALYs). DALYs measure the total burden of disease – both from years of life lost due to premature death and years lived with a disability. One DALY equals one lost year of healthy life. Here it is age-standardized and expressed per 100,000 individuals.
caption:
The burden of disease vs. GDP per capita
The burden of disease is measured in Disability-Adjusted Life Years (DALYs). DALYs measure the total burden of disease – both from years of life lost due to premature death and years lived with a disability. One DALY equals one lost year of healthy life. Here it is age-standardized and expressed per 100,000 individuals.

This scatter plot maps the relationship between national economic output and health outcomes for 2023, plotting GDP per capita on a logarithmic horizontal axis against the burden of disease on a linear vertical axis. The vertical axis measures Disability-Adjusted Life Years (DALYs) per 100,000 people, ranging from 0 to 80,000, while the horizontal axis displays GDP per capita in international dollars at 2021 prices, spanning from $2,000 to $100,000. Circle sizes correspond to population, with a legend indicating scales from 600 million to 1.4 billion. The data points form a distinct downward curve, showing that higher income levels coincide with lower disease burdens. Countries in the lowest income bracket, such as the Central African Republic, Niger, Chad, and Burundi, cluster in the upper-left quadrant with DALY rates between 60,000 and 80,000. Moving rightward along the income scale, nations like Nigeria, India, and Pakistan occupy the middle range with DALY rates roughly between 30,000 and 60,000. At the far right, high-income economies including the United States, Germany, Japan, and Singapore group tightly in the bottom-right corner, all falling below the 30,000 DALY mark. The largest circles, representing China and India, sit in the middle of the distribution, while smaller circles for European and North American nations populate the low-burden, high-income zone. The visual arrangement demonstrates a consistent inverse association where increases in GDP per capita align with reductions in the total years of healthy life lost to disease and disability.
